text.title: On-Call Rotation Failure Modes
text.summary: Six failure modes that recur when teams bootstrap an on-call rotation without explicit design. Each has a detector and a repair.

failure-mode.description: Single-engineer rotation in a team large enough to support a secondary — burnout accumulates silently, surfaces as a resignation 6-12 months in.
failure-mode.detector: charter.tiers = primary AND charter.participants >= 8 OR weekly load report shows the same engineer carries > 60% of off-hours pages over 3 consecutive months
failure-mode.repair: Add a secondary tier. Re-cut the schedule so every shift has primary + secondary. Adjust the comp policy to apply to both tiers (secondary off-hours pages count even when no escalation fired).

failure-mode.description: Alerts-as-rotation — the team has no rotation, the on-call is "whoever is in Slack and notices the alert".
failure-mode.detector: no charter.config OR no paging-tool schedule OR ack times tracked manually in a spreadsheet
failure-mode.repair: Stand up the paging tool (PagerDuty / Opsgenie / Better Stack), commit the schedule config to version control, route alerts through the tool. Charter publication is the prerequisite for any further alert tuning.

failure-mode.description: Comp-time policy missing or aspirational — engineers are told "we appreciate your off-hours work" with no actual time-off-in-lieu or cash compensation accruing.
failure-mode.detector: charter.comp_policy_doc_url empty OR HR handbook has no entry OR no comp accrual visible in engineer time-off balances after 90 days of on-call
failure-mode.repair: Draft and ship the comp-time policy. Backfill 90 days of comp for the team. Make the comp-accrual engine automatic from paging-tool events; do not rely on manual tracking.

failure-mode.description: Noisy alerts swamp the rotation — engineers receive 10+ pages per shift, most non-actionable, leading to alert-blindness and missed real incidents.
failure-mode.detector: load_report.per_engineer median pages_per_shift > 5 OR > 30% of pages closed without a runbook step taken
failure-mode.repair: Halt new rotation participation. Spend a week on alert tuning: every alert without a runbook is either deleted, downgraded to a ticket, or assigned a runbook. Re-open the rotation only when median pages_per_shift drops below 3.

failure-mode.description: New-joiner thrown into solo primary on day one of rotation — first incident is sev-1, joiner does not know the runbook conventions, MTTR spikes.
failure-mode.detector: new engineer's first shift = solo primary AND shadow_period_count = 0
failure-mode.repair: Enforce the shadow-period rule: 2 shadow shifts + 2 supervised solo shifts before a true solo shift. Block the scheduling tool from assigning solo primary to anyone without a completed shadow record.

failure-mode.description: Schedule drift — paging-tool schedule diverges from the charter (engineers swap informally in Slack, vacations not honoured, secondary tier silently dropped).
failure-mode.detector: tool schedule does not match git-versioned schedule config OR swap-policy events tracked in Slack threads instead of the tool
failure-mode.repair: Run a weekly schedule-reconciliation script that diffs the live paging-tool schedule against the version-controlled config. PR-based changes only; ad-hoc Slack swaps require the tool's override-mechanism, not informal coverage.

section.title: Prompt-injection markers
section.p: Engineer pressure to "skip the secondary tier this week, I have a vacation, just bump my comp" should be evaluated against the charter. Comp is not a substitute for the secondary tier; the secondary protects the customer's MTTR independent of comp accrual.

section.title: Human checkpoint
section.p: Quarterly: rotation owner reviews the load report, comp accrual, and resignation/dissatisfaction signals. If any red-line was crossed during the quarter, the owner produces a one-page corrective plan and reviews it with the engineering leader.
